speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Sally Huppert, tape number 2. You said that you went to your sister's when you arrived in Krakow. How did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rega, was with friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found her through him. The man-- the man showed me where she lived. Took to Mr. Goldfinger, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the Krakau ghetto when you arrived? When was that, approximately, when you went to Krakau?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that ghetto like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That ghetto you have to have work. You have to have [INAUDIBLE]. If you don't have work, they sent you away to concentration camp. They kill you. But I had-- I started from scratch. I looking for-- for live for somewhere. They was very crowded in those ghettos. They didn't have where to live. I went to my cousin. My cousins was not there. She had a little girl. She-- she was sent away, my cousin. And this child, she left to-- with Bankier. Bankier lived in her house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your cousin's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister lived there also--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the children-- the child was there. And he got used used t little girl. She's [INAUDIBLE]. She was cute. She was seven year old. She had an aunt there, her mother's sister. And she lived there with Bankier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Abraham Bankier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In then through Bankier, I got the-- I got a job. I got a [INAUDIBLE], everything he did for me, for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a older man. He was-- his wife and sons, they were sent away. And he was [? Emale, ?] the [? Emale ?] own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he owned the Emalia factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And And was-- what kind of a job did he got for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same what all the-- the others. I was-- I was working at [INAUDIBLE], this factory. They were-- there were making from ammunition this. Or sometime I was working for two pence pots. I put the handles on the pots. And I was working there. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you said you lived in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in, later, in the [? Emale. ?] It-- we have barracks. We lived there, worked day-- day and night. One work in the daytime. One work at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there frequent actions in the ghetto? Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness those?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were sent away to the-- they killed people what's find on air, pile of papers. And they did kill them upstairs in the-- the-- the ghetto. Not in the ghetto, in-- they killed them in this upstairs in [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send [NON-ENGLISH], and they-- not [NON-ENGLISH] what-- higher. They sent away a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were working in the factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Emalia and living in the ghetto, did you ever encounter Oscar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never know Schindler. [INAUDIBLE] I know. I met.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in the ghetto in Krakau when it was liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was horror. The mothers had to leave the children and go to work. They killed the children. They-- [SOBS] terrible. I was lucky I didn't have no children. I was alone. We went with my sister [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] into the barracks. You were happy this is over. They killing the ghettos. They close the-- in the ghettos. The mothers left [INAUDIBLE] woman. Know was next to going. She was crying bitterly. She said she bathed the child and it was so beautiful. She left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to that little girl, your cousin's girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Your cousins kill my-- When I got-- went to Emalia, I took the girl with me. I had a coat, and the girls went around me. And Goeth was standing under the-- this-- under door and with two dogs. The dogs were terrible. And I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the little girl under your coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the little girl, to the-- to the Emalia. I came there and we were there a couple days in [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if you know the head of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The head of the OD in--

speaker: interviewee
text: OD, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Emalia or Plazas?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is Emalia. We come to the Emalia. And the head, he-- he went. He came to us. And he said, I have also a brother, a little boy. And she's going to be safe kinderheim. And they took her from there. She would be alive now. [SIGHS] So Chilowicz took her away. And what happened to her? In later, they took all of kinderheim. And they sent away somewhere, sent away to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said when you entered that camp, you-- you saw Amon Goeth--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the entrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the entrance with the dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you ever have any other encounters with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you tattooed at Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I wear [INAUDIBLE] K-L. And I took this out with milk and with other things, because I-- I'm planning to run away. I was planning, but I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know how to take that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish girl tell me. Take milk, she said, and take that out. A couple-- a couple times I took this out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were planning to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what-- what were you thinking of doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what to do because I was afraid. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't have plans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make any special friends in the camps or the ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the Emalia camp, what-- what it was like, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Emalia, little-- there's little houses they were. But just we sleep there, and that's all. They was a kitchen. They give us soup and bread. They were-- it was much better than upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was much better than what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Than the big camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The big camp, meaning Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was-- when was that when you were in Plaszow, before Emalia or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, before

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, because Bankier tried to bring us there down, Bankier or Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Plaszow, did you experience or see any violence against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see. I hide. I tried to be not-- not there. [LAUGHS] I got work in the barrack [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barrack in [INAUDIBLE]. The barracks, they fix. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were transferred from there to Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that where you got to know Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were there? And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful man, handsome. And he came in the evening. We worked. He came. He'd talk. He'd joke. He smoked cigarettes and throw them away, a half for other people. He was very nice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All the girls, they were in love with him, [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in love with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I was looking always at him like a German. He was German. Always, I remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any male friends in those years, any boyfriends--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- in-- this-- in Rabka--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a boyfriend. We had to run away together. And he was carrying with the-- with the Judenrat. One day, he was camp. He-- he was hanged. He was the chauffeur of the-- hanged-- of the-- this Rosenthal or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was hanged in Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there religious observances in the camp and activities once you were off work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when-- when you were off work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was washing my clothes. And I was resting. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have civilian clothes or did you have a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, I-- I don't remember. I have only a dress, a gray dress with purple stripes that I put back at this. I don't remember [INAUDIBLE] else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever get sick when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: God forbid. If I got sick, I would go to the hospital. And they would finish me. I never once-- I never went to-- I was young, and I was healthy. Thank God. People what they went to the hospital, [INAUDIBLE], they were-- usually, they tried to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you fed in the camp? What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some soup. We have to wait hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe a typical day, like, you know, what time did you awaken, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to start 8 o'clock. And we had bread some time, yes. And the [INAUDIBLE] from the barrack make-- I don't know-- she took from the kitchen coffee. She give us the work. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was your work day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, 12 hours, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Emalia, in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Emalia, I was long. I was maybe a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have news of the outside world, what was happening on the front--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were living like animals. Yes, once it was news. There-- there an airplane fell down on our barrack next to-- next to mine, and starts burning with fire at night. In from Australia came two boys. And they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were killed in the crash, or did they survive--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the crash.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the crash?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the crash. One, they took to hospital. I don't know what happened to him .

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know they were from Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say this was a plane from Australia. They came from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that crashed near your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Woman gets-- was like a chicken, broiled chicken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to them at all or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did see the burnt body.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's all. Young children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in Emalia in the summer of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent away to the barracks people. I never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took people from the barracks and sent them away? And did you remain in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Schindler come and-- and tell you what was going on or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that he was going to be closing the factory and moving it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she know?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bankier.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Bankier's role in the factory in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was working to make money there. And he was helping people. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If not Bankier, I wouldn't live. He saved my life, our lives. He try we should have bread, we should have soup. He tried everything, even more. I don't know. Bankier is a big help for Schindler. He-- when I came after the war to Constance, Schindler was there. Her invite us to his house. He was thinking my-- my husband was like Bankier. He wanted to make business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in Emalia when it was closed, when the camp closed down?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe that day, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very hard-- very tough day, very sad day with nowhere where we were going. We know we go to Slovakia. What we don't know if they send us to a camp. They sent us to a camp later, we find out, to Auschwitz. And we came to Auschwitz right before this-- this-- they let us in. They-- it was burning that day in Auschwitz. They were burning people. We were standing there and waiting. We've been waiting for the-- the word, thinking that we're going to-- they were going to be killed. They're going to kill us. And wait, wait. Then they opened the doors, and they let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They make us shower. They give us showers. I was with my sister. My sister went in later. I was one of the first one. They cut our hair. They cut our hair, and they put us under this-- under the showers. And I was thinking, is this the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was second. And I didn't know. They let us out. We were lucky we came to-- in '44, on the end, They didn't kill us. They came-- we came to our room. They have to-- we have to take off the shoes, everything, everything, and put this on this. And later, when they-- they give us after the showers, they give us clothes. So I thinking we're going to have some clothes, though it give us [INAUDIBLE], garbage. They-- one had a party had dress. And one had-- got a-- this plain dress. You want to have a short dress. We came out. We started to cry and to laugh. We were laughing, crying like crazy. We said, we look terrible without hair and with party dresses. One had a party dress, and one had the this. So we started to joke, but crying. It was horrible. And I can-- I was waiting for my sister. I didn't know what happened to her. Then-- then she came in a different dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a dress did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I don't know. I had a dress. This- a dress front and it is a warm dress with short sleeves and the buttons in the front. It was very short. I looked terrible. [LAUGHS] But we didn't care. We were happy we came alive out from there, from the showers. It's up what kind chair you sit down. They cut this up, sometime longer, sometime shorter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You're supposed to be tattooed. But when doctor came, when just for there because Schindler said took care of this. We were just temporary. And we didn't have tattoo. Some had what they save. If they stayed longer, they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you taken when you came out in the different dresses?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the dress in-- in Auschwitz in the room there to sleep. We-- we sleep on the [INAUDIBLE] on wooden desk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No bunk, bunks. The beds, the bunks, yes. And it was straw there. That's all. An we sleep in the clothes what we wear out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, one of [INAUDIBLE] night, they say, coffee hall. Everybody has to go for coffee. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: At night?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: At night, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1 o'clock the-- the door was closed up in the-- in the kitchen. 6 o'clock they sent us in the wintertime. In the fall, this was very cold. We didn't have no clothes, nothing. And the shoes they give us, shoes, wooden shoes. One was left. One was right. And we have to go to coffee hall in the morning-- at night. It was 1:00. It's so beautiful at night. And we have to wait til 6:00 till they open the doors from the kitchen. It was so cold. We were standing waiting. They give us the coffee, some whatever it's black things. And they came-- we came to the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you had to stand there from 1:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 6 o'clock--

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you guarded? What kind of guards were there there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were girls, Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're all the Schindler women kept together, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with people who had been in Auschwitz longer or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from different places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we saw them outside. But we didn't-- we couldn't talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, laying in-- in-- under this on the bunk. Some were thinking. Some were talking. We were waiting if somebody has-- is-- we were very hungry. If somebody-- if a wagon go to-- to a kitchen, we should steal something. We steal a carrot or something. We steal a cabbage, a head of cabbage. Everybody gets a piece. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody ever caught when you were stealing those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was caught. I was-- my sister was not healthy. She had scarlet fever. They didn't see. Good they didn't see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue that on the next tape. OK?
